Matthew 26 unfolds in terrifying sequence: the conspiracy to kill Jesus, the betrayal for thirty pieces of silver, the Last Supper where Christ announces His imminent death, the midnight arrest in Gethsemane, and the trial where He is condemned to die. Yet woven through this darkness is a quiet recognition of what is actually happening. Jesus knows. He announces it plainly at the beginning—"the Son of Man will be handed over to be crucified"—and He moves toward it with deliberate purpose. This is not tragedy befalling an unprepared victim. When the woman anoints Him with expensive perfume, Jesus understands: she is preparing His body for burial. When He breaks bread and pours wine at Passover, He is interpreting His own death as the fulfillment of Israel's redemptive story. "This is My blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins." The One being betrayed is simultaneously the One whose betrayal accomplishes the world's salvation.

Yet Matthew shows us the actual cost of this moment—not sanitized, not distant. The disciples fall asleep in the garden while Jesus wrestles in prayer so intensely that He sweats blood. Peter, who swears he would die with Jesus, denies even knowing Him. Not once, but three times, with curses and oaths, as a servant girl and then bystanders press him. When the rooster crows, Peter remembers Jesus' words and weeps bitterly. This is the moment where bold declarations shatter against human fear. The disciples scatter. The one who should have stood with Jesus stands instead with His executioners. And yet—this too is written, predicted, known. Jesus told them plainly: "You will all fall away on account of Me." Not because they are uniquely weak, but because they are human, and the weight of what is coming breaks everyone who tries to carry it alone.

What grips us in this chapter is the collision between human plotting and divine purpose. The chief priests scheme to arrest Jesus covertly so there is no riot. Judas negotiates a price. The Sanhedrin scrambles to manufacture false testimony. They are so certain they are in control, so sure of their power to silence this threat. But every strand they weave has already been written. Every betrayal, every denial, every blow and taunt—it is all caught within a larger narrative that Jesus sees and accepts. He does not fight it. He walks toward it. He prepares His disciples for it. And in doing so, He transforms what looks like the victory of evil into the very mechanism by which sin and death are defeated. This is the scandal of the gospel: that our betrayals and failures, our denials and scatterings, do not thwart God's purpose. They become part of it. That does not excuse them. But it does mean that even our worst moments cannot separate us from the One who is walking toward the cross precisely because He loves us, precisely to forgive us, precisely to make a way for us to come home.
